slides.heading: European Expansion, Conquest and the Slave Trade
slides.lead: How Europe's overseas expansion from the 1400s reshaped the Americas, Africa and the Cape - and built a system of slavery whose consequences are still felt today.

image.alt: A sailing caravel crossing a stylised ocean between three landmasses, suggesting Atlantic connections.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#eef3f6'/><rect y='118' width='320' height='62' fill='#3a6b8a'/><path d='M18 118 h70 v40 h-70 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M232 108 h72 v50 h-72 Z' fill='#c8a86a'/><path d='M120 96 q22 -10 44 0 v18 q-22 -8 -44 0 Z' fill='#8a5a3a'/><rect x='140' y='58' width='3' height='40' fill='#5c3a24'/><path d='M143 60 l26 16 l-26 12 Z' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M143 60 l-22 14 l22 10 Z' fill='#dcd3c2'/></svg>

slides.notes: Frame the topic. This is a global-history unit that connects Europe, the Americas, West Africa and the Cape. Stress the two linked processes learners will trace: (1) European expansion and conquest, and (2) the Atlantic slave trade that grew out of it. Preview that we will end at the Cape, where this global story reaches South Africa. Set an analytical tone appropriate to FET: we look for causes, consequences and different perspectives, not just events.

slides.heading: The world around 1500
slides.lead: Europe was not the centre of the world in 1500 - it was one of several wealthy, powerful regions.

blocks.text: In 1500 major centres of power and trade existed across the globe: the **Ming** in China, the **Mughal** and other states in India, the **Ottoman Empire** across the eastern Mediterranean, the wealthy **Songhai Empire** in West Africa, and the **Aztec** and **Inca** empires in the Americas.

items: Long-distance trade in gold, spices, textiles and enslaved people already linked Africa, Asia and Europe.
European states were relatively small and wanted direct access to the rich Asian spice trade.
The Ottomans controlled the overland routes east, pushing Europeans to look for a sea route.

slides.notes: Correct the common misconception that Europe was uniquely advanced. Emphasise that Europeans expanded partly because they were on the edge of existing trade networks and wanted in. Naming African and American empires up front sets up the later point that conquest destroyed sophisticated societies, not 'empty' lands. This is a good place to draw a rough world map on the board.

slides.heading: Why did Europe expand overseas?
slides.lead: Historians often summarise the motives as 'God, gold and glory' - but new technology made expansion possible.

blocks.heading: Gold (wealth)
blocks.text: Direct access to spices, gold and new markets promised huge profits for merchants and monarchs.

blocks.heading: God (religion)
blocks.text: Rulers and the Church wanted to spread Christianity and compete with the Muslim world.

blocks.heading: Glory (power)
blocks.text: Portugal, Spain and later others competed for prestige, territory and rival advantage.

blocks.heading: Technology
blocks.text: The caravel, compass, astrolabe and better maps and cannon made long ocean voyages possible.

slides.notes: Use the 'God, gold, glory' phrase as a memory hook but push learners to weigh which motive mattered most - a typical FET analytical skill. Add that technology (means) is different from motive (reason): motives existed earlier, but new ships and navigation tools turned ambition into action. Ask learners to rank the four factors and justify their choice.

slides.heading: Portugal and Spain lead the way
slides.lead: Two small Atlantic kingdoms opened sea routes that reordered global trade.

blocks.text: **Portugal** pushed south along the African coast: Bartolomeu **Dias** rounded the Cape of Good Hope in 1488, and **Vasco da Gama** reached India by sea in 1498. **Spain** sailed west: in 1492 **Christopher Columbus** reached the Caribbean, believing he had found a route to Asia.

items: The Portuguese route around Africa gave Europe direct access to Asian spices.
Columbus's voyages opened the Americas to European invasion and settlement.
In 1494 the Treaty of Tordesillas divided the non-European world between Portugal and Spain.

image.alt: A compass rose beside a curved dotted sea route running past the tip of Africa.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#eef3f6'/><path d='M150 20 q40 40 20 90 q-10 30 -40 40' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='6 6'/><path d='M130 30 h50 v70 q-20 30 -50 30 Z' fill='#c8a86a' opacity='0.6'/><g transform='translate(52,80)'><circle r='30' fill='none' stroke='#3a6b8a' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M0 -30 L7 0 L0 30 L-7 0 Z' fill='#3a6b8a'/><path d='M-30 0 L0 -7 L30 0 L0 7 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><text x='0' y='-34' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle' fill='#333'>N</text></g></svg>

slides.notes: Keep dates as anchors but focus on significance: the Portuguese route (round Africa) and the Spanish route (across the Atlantic) created two different empires. Note the irony in 'discovery' language - da Gama and Columbus reached lands already home to millions. The Treaty of Tordesillas shows Europeans dividing up other people's lands without consent. Link forward: the Cape sea route explains why Europeans later settled at the Cape.

slides.heading: The fall of the Aztec and Inca empires
slides.lead: Within a generation, small Spanish forces toppled two of the world's largest empires.

blocks.text: Spanish **conquistadors** invaded the mainland Americas. **Hernan Cortes** overthrew the **Aztec Empire** (in present-day Mexico) between 1519 and 1521, and **Francisco Pizarro** conquered the **Inca Empire** (in present-day Peru) from 1532.

items: The Spanish seized vast quantities of gold and silver.
Conquered land and people were parcelled out to settlers under the encomienda system.
Silver from mines such as Potosi flowed into the global economy through Spain.

slides.notes: Give learners the scale: a few hundred Spaniards defeated empires of millions. That paradox is the hinge of the next slide. Introduce encomienda as forced labour that foreshadows later slavery. Avoid glorifying conquistadors - use the word 'invaded' rather than 'discovered'. Potosi silver is a good example of how conquest fed a new global economy.

slides.heading: How did so few conquer so many?
slides.lead: Superior weapons alone do not explain it - disease and alliances were decisive.

blocks.text: Historians point to several combined factors rather than any single cause. The most devastating was **disease**: Europeans carried smallpox, measles and other illnesses to which Indigenous Americans had no immunity.

items: Epidemics killed a very large share of the Indigenous population, weakening resistance.
Steel weapons, guns, armour and horses gave the Spanish an advantage in battle.
The Spanish exploited rivalries, allying with peoples who resented Aztec or Inca rule.

image.alt: Three stacked bars showing disease as the largest factor, then weapons, then alliances.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#eef3f6'/><rect x='30' y='40' width='150' height='22' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='30' y='74' width='96' height='22' fill='#c8894a'/><rect x='30' y='108' width='70' height='22' fill='#7a8b5a'/><text x='34' y='56' font-size='11' fill='#fff'>Disease</text><text x='130' y='90' font-size='11' fill='#5c3a24'>Weapons</text><text x='104' y='124' font-size='11' fill='#3a5a2a'>Alliances</text></svg>

slides.notes: This is the key analytical slide. Push back on the 'European superiority' story: the single biggest killer was disease, which was accidental, not a sign of a superior civilisation. Weapons and horses helped in battle, and local alliances provided most of the fighting force. Ask learners to explain why relying on one cause gives a misleading picture of history. [SME] Population-loss figures for the Americas vary widely between historians - keep to 'a very large share' unless a source specifies a range.

slides.heading: Catastrophe and a demand for labour
slides.lead: Conquest brought demographic collapse - and created the labour shortage that drove the slave trade.

blocks.text: The Indigenous population fell sharply through disease, war and forced labour. As Europeans set up **plantations** and mines to grow sugar, tobacco and mine silver, they demanded a large, controllable workforce.

items: Sugar plantations in the Caribbean and Brazil were extremely profitable but needed huge amounts of labour.
With Indigenous numbers collapsing, Europeans turned to enslaved Africans.
This choice linked the Americas, Europe and Africa into one Atlantic system.

slides.notes: Draw the causal chain explicitly: conquest -> population collapse -> labour shortage -> demand for enslaved African labour. This is the bridge from the 'conquest' half of the unit to the 'slave trade' half. Note that plantation profits, especially from sugar, were the economic engine. Emphasise that enslaving Africans was a deliberate choice made for profit, not an inevitability.

slides.heading: A forced migration of millions
slides.lead: Over roughly 350 years, an estimated 12 million Africans were forced across the Atlantic.

blocks.text: European traders bought or captured people in West and Central Africa and shipped them to the Americas to work as **enslaved labour**. Enslaved people were treated in law as property that could be bought, sold and inherited.

items: Around 12 million Africans were embarked; roughly 10 to 11 million survived the crossing.
Some African rulers and merchants took part in supplying captives, often through warfare.
The trade drained Africa of people and fuelled conflict, while enriching European ports.

image.alt: Arrows showing large numbers of people moved from West Africa across the Atlantic to the Americas.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#eef3f6'/><path d='M40 40 h44 v70 h-44 Z' fill='#c8a86a'/><path d='M156 30 h44 v90 h-44 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M150 74 q-30 -18 -62 0' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4' marker-end='url(#a)'/><defs><marker id='a' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L8 4 L0 8 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/></marker></defs><text x='150' y='96' font-size='9' fill='#333'>Africa</text><text x='42' y='126' font-size='9' fill='#333'>Americas</text></svg>

slides.notes: Handle numbers responsibly: cite the widely used estimate of about 12 million embarked. Make clear the human meaning behind the figure - each number was a person with a family and a home. Address African involvement honestly but with nuance: some African states participated, often under pressure or for their own gain, but Europeans created and controlled the demand. Avoid framing that shifts blame away from the European-run system. [SME] Confirm the 12 million / 10-11 million survival figures against the Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database or a cited SAHO source before production.

slides.heading: The triangular trade
slides.lead: Ships followed a three-sided route that turned enslaved people into profit at every stage.

blocks.text: The **triangular trade** linked three regions. Manufactured goods went from Europe to Africa; enslaved Africans were shipped across the Atlantic (the **Middle Passage**); and plantation products like sugar, tobacco and cotton returned to Europe.

items: Leg 1: guns, cloth and metal goods from Europe to the West African coast.
Leg 2: enslaved people from Africa to the Americas - the Middle Passage.
Leg 3: sugar, rum, tobacco and cotton from the Americas back to Europe.

image.alt: A triangle linking Europe, Africa and the Americas showing the three legs of the trade.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 170' role='img'><rect width='240' height='170' fill='#eef3f6'/><circle cx='170' cy='34' r='14' fill='#3a6b8a'/><circle cx='196' cy='120' r='14' fill='#c8a86a'/><circle cx='44' cy='120' r='14' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M158 44 L58 112' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M58 128 L182 128' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M188 108 L176 48' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='3'/><text x='170' y='20' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle' fill='#333'>Europe</text><text x='210' y='140' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle' fill='#333'>Africa</text><text x='30' y='140' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle' fill='#333'>Americas</text></svg>

slides.notes: Walk the three legs on the diagram. Stress that the 'goods' on the second leg were human beings - this is where a diagram can hide horror, so name it. Point out that profit was made at each corner, which is why so many European merchants and cities backed the system. This slide sets up the Middle Passage, treated closely next.

slides.heading: The Middle Passage
slides.lead: The Atlantic crossing was a brutal ordeal that many did not survive.

blocks.text: On the **Middle Passage**, enslaved people were packed tightly below deck for voyages that could last many weeks. Conditions were overcrowded and unsanitary, and disease, cruelty and despair caused many deaths.

items: People were chained, given little food and water, and denied basic dignity.
A significant proportion died during the crossing from disease and ill-treatment.
Despite this, enslaved people resisted - through refusing food, revolts and holding on to memory and culture.

slides.notes: This is the most sensitive slide - pitch it honestly but without gratuitous detail. The aim is empathy and understanding, not shock. Acknowledge that this history can be upsetting, especially for learners whose ancestors were affected, and create space for respectful discussion. End on human agency: enslaved people were not only victims but also resisted and preserved culture, which leads into the next slide. Avoid graphic description; focus on the injustice and the humanity of those enslaved.

slides.heading: Life, resistance and survival
slides.lead: Enslaved people were never simply passive - they resisted in many ways and shaped new cultures.

blocks.text: In the Americas, enslaved people faced forced labour and violence on plantations, yet they resisted **openly and quietly** and built new communities and identities.

items: Open resistance included revolts, escape and forming free communities of escaped people.
Everyday resistance included working slowly, breaking tools and keeping languages, music and religion alive.
The Haitian Revolution (1791-1804) saw enslaved people overthrow slavery and found a free state.

image.alt: A broken chain link, symbolising resistance to slavery.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 140' role='img'><rect width='220' height='140' fill='#eef3f6'/><g fill='none' stroke='#5c3a24' stroke-width='9'><ellipse cx='70' cy='70' rx='22' ry='14'/><ellipse cx='150' cy='70' rx='22' ry='14'/></g><path d='M96 70 h14' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='9'/><path d='M110 70 l14 0' stroke='#eef3f6' stroke-width='13'/><path d='M112 58 l10 10 l-10 10' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4'/></svg>

slides.notes: Balance the suffering of the previous slide with agency here - this is important for a respectful, accurate account. Distinguish open resistance (revolts, marronage) from everyday resistance (go-slows, cultural survival). The Haitian Revolution is a powerful example of enslaved people winning freedom and shaping world history. This slide also models the FET skill of recognising perspective and agency, not just victimhood.

slides.heading: Slavery at the Cape
slides.lead: When the Dutch settled at the Cape in 1652, they built a slave society drawing on the Indian Ocean world.

blocks.text: The **Dutch East India Company (VOC)** established a refreshment station at the Cape in 1652. From 1658 it imported enslaved people - mainly from East Africa, Madagascar, India and Southeast Asia - to work as labourers, artisans and domestic workers.

items: Cape slavery drew on the Indian Ocean trade, not mainly the Atlantic trade.
The Company's Slave Lodge in Cape Town held many of the VOC's enslaved people.
Britain abolished the slave trade in 1807 and abolished slavery at the Cape from 1834, followed by four years of 'apprenticeship'.

slides.notes: Localise the global story: South Africa's Cape was part of the same age of expansion and slavery, but supplied largely through the Indian Ocean rather than the Atlantic. This distinction is often examined. Note the diverse origins of Cape slaves, which shaped Cape culture, language (Afrikaans) and society. The Slave Lodge in Cape Town is a concrete, visitable site learners may know. Flag the long shadow: emancipation in 1834-1838 did not bring equality, linking to later South African history.

slides.heading: Expansion, conquest and slavery, 1488-1838

blocks.alt: A timeline running from Dias rounding the Cape in 1488 to the end of slavery apprenticeship at the Cape in 1838.

events.label: Dias rounds the Cape of Good Hope
Da Gama reaches India by sea
Dutch settle at the Cape; slavery follows
Slavery ended at the Cape, then apprenticeship

slides.notes: Use the timeline to consolidate the whole unit and to show how tightly the events connect across a single long period. Ask learners to identify the causal links between entries (for example, how conquest in the Americas relates to the growth of the slave trade). Point out that abolition of the trade (1807) came before abolition of slavery itself (1830s), a distinction often tested.

slides.heading: Pulling it together
slides.lead: European expansion, the conquest of the Americas and the Atlantic slave trade were parts of one connected process with lasting consequences.

blocks.text: From the 1400s, European expansion linked continents into new networks of trade and power. Conquest devastated the Americas and created a demand for labour that drove the **Atlantic slave trade**, while at the Cape a slave society took shape through the Indian Ocean world. These processes shaped the modern world - and remain a subject of debate and remembrance today.

blocks.label: Q1
blocks.question: Explain why European states expanded overseas from the 1400s. Refer to at least three different motives in your answer.

blocks.label: Q2
blocks.question: 'Disease, not superior weapons, was the main reason the Spanish conquered the Americas.' Do you agree? Support your view with evidence.

blocks.label: Q3
blocks.question: Describe how the conquest of the Americas was connected to the growth of the Atlantic slave trade.

slides.notes: Close by reinforcing the through-line: expansion, conquest and slavery were connected, not separate topics. The three questions mirror CAPS FET styles - a structured explanation (Q1), a source-supported argument or essay-style judgement (Q2), and a cause-and-consequence link (Q3). Remind learners to use evidence and to consider more than one perspective. Note that this unit leads into later Grade 10 work on the Cape colonial economy and the making of South African society.
